methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

path: Selector check on userOp.callData[0:4] for executeWithoutChainIdValidation
Hash recomputed without chain ID if cross-chain op, else use entrypoint-supplied userOpHash
_validateSignature() called with the hash and userOp.signature
Dispatches to ECDSA or WebAuthn verification path based on owner bytes length
transformations: userOpHash recomputed without chainId for replayable ops: keccak256(abi.encode(UserOperationLib.hash(userOp), entryPoint()))
For passkey owners: WebAuthn.verify with challenge = abi.encode(message)
data_flows.trust_changes: Bundler-submitted data transitions to trusted after signature verification; ownerIndex is trusted implicitly (attacker can choose any index, but signature must match)
validation_points: Public key on-curve check in FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve
Signature malleability check (s <= n/2) in WebAuthn.verify
WebAuthn type and challenge field verification
exposure_risks: ownerIndex is user-controlled; wrong index causes revert at signature check, not a security risk but may waste gas
manipulation_risks: Cross-chain replay: userOpHash is recomputed without chain ID for whitelisted selectors, enabling intentional replay across chains
Attacker controlling typeIndex/challengeIndex in WebAuthn could attempt string position manipulation, but keccak256 match and full-JSON hashing prevent bypass

path: replaySafeHash() wraps hash with EIP-712 domain separator (chainId + address(this))
Same owner-based dispatch as UserOp validation
transformations: Domain separator includes block.chainid and address(this)
data_flows.trust_changes: External callers receive boolean validity; anti-replay layer prevents cross-account/cross-chain signature reuse
validation_points: EIP-712 domain separator binds to specific chain and contract address
Same signature verification as UserOp path
manipulation_risks: If a dApp does not use replaySafeHash to construct the message, signatures may fail or the anti-replay protection is bypassed conceptually

data_flows.source: UserOperation with paymasterAndData pointing to MagicSpend
path: _validateRequest: nonce uniqueness check, nonce marked used, event emitted
Signature validated via isValidWithdrawSignature (owner's signature on getHash())
withdrawable sent to account via forceSafeTransferETH
transformations: withdrawAmount split into gas portion (maxCost) and user portion (withdrawAmount - maxCost)
Gas refund computed as maxGasCost - actualGasCost in postOp
Hash computed via EIP-191 v0x45: toEthSignedMessageHash(abi.encode(this, account, chainid, asset, amount, nonce, expiry))
sinks: ETH transferred to user account in postOp
data_flows.trust_changes: Signature by MagicSpend owner authorizes the withdrawal; EntryPoint is trusted to call postOp with correct gas cost data
validation_points: Expiry encoded in validationData for EntryPoint to enforce
exposure_risks: Nonce state is permanently consumed even if validation returns sigFailed=1
manipulation_risks: Balance check race: multiple UserOps in the same bundle each pass balance check independently during validation phase, but combined withdrawals may exceed actual balance
Nonce is consumed in _validateRequest before signature check in validatePaymasterUserOp - invalid signature still burns the nonce

path: onlyOwner modifier: _checkOwner() verifies isOwnerAddress(msg.sender) or msg.sender == address(this)
For add: _addOwner encodes bytes, checks AlreadyOwner, stores in ownerAtIndex[nextOwnerIndex++] and isOwner[bytes]
For remove: loads ownerAtIndex[index], checks non-empty, deletes isOwner[bytes] and ownerAtIndex[index]
sinks: MultiOwnableStorage: ownerAtIndex mapping, isOwner mapping, nextOwnerIndex counter
data_flows.trust_changes: Only existing owners can modify the owner set; self-call (address(this)) also authorized
exposure_risks: Removed owner index leaves a gap that is never reused - nextOwnerIndex only increments
manipulation_risks: Cross-chain replay: owner management functions are whitelisted for chain-agnostic execution, so adding/removing owners replays across all chains
No minimum owner count: all owners can be removed, permanently locking the wallet

data_flows.source: User provides WebAuthnAuth struct via signature
path: Type verification: clientDataJSON.slice(typeIndex, typeIndex+21) must match expected type hash
Challenge verification: clientDataJSON.slice(challengeIndex, challengeIndex+len) must match base64url-encoded challenge
Optional UV flag check at authenticatorData[32]
transformations: challenge -> base64url encoding -> embedded in expected challenge string
authenticatorData + sha256(clientDataJSON) -> sha256 -> messageHash for ECDSA verification
sinks: Boolean verification result returned to caller
data_flows.trust_changes: User-supplied typeIndex and challengeIndex are validated by content comparison, not by position; authenticatorData comes from hardware authenticator and is validated via signature
validation_points: ECDSA signature verification on message hash
manipulation_risks: typeIndex and challengeIndex are user-controlled; attacker could point them to positions within other JSON fields that happen to contain matching substrings, but since the full clientDataJSON is hashed and signed this cannot forge a signature
RIP-7212 precompile fallback: if 0x100 has unrelated code deployed on certain chains, staticcall could return unexpected data; ret.length > 0 check partially mitigates

transformations: owners + nonce -> keccak256 -> salt for CREATE2
ERC1967 proxy deployed with implementation slot set
data_flows.trust_changes: First deployer sets the owners; subsequent calls to same salt return existing address without re-initialization
exposure_risks: Anyone can deploy the account for any set of owners; first deployer pays gas but cannot influence ownership
manipulation_risks: If msg.value is sent during createAccount for an already-deployed account, the ETH is sent to the existing proxy via createDeterministicERC1967 - this is by design

transformations: WithdrawRequest fields -> getHash() -> EIP-191 signed message hash
sinks: ETH or ERC20 transferred to msg.sender
data_flows.trust_changes: MagicSpend owner's signature authorizes the withdrawal for a specific account
manipulation_risks: Nonce is consumed before signature check - if signature is invalid, nonce is still burned (DoS if attacker has the withdraw request data but not a valid signature, they can front-run and burn the nonce for the legitimate user)

core_rules: Smart wallet controlled by multiple owners (EOA addresses or passkey public keys)
Only EntryPoint can call validateUserOp; only EntryPoint or owners can execute transactions
Cross-chain replayable operations limited to owner management and upgrade functions
MagicSpend withdrawals require owner signature with nonce, expiry, and chain-binding
UUPS upgrade only allowed by wallet owners
ERC-1271 signatures protected against cross-account replay via EIP-712 domain separator
economic_incentives: MagicSpend sponsors gas by providing ETH via paymaster; excess refunded to user in postOp
Bundlers are incentivized to include UserOps with valid signatures and sufficient gas
MagicSpend owner can withdraw any funds from the contract
governance_mechanisms: Multi-owner model: any single owner can add/remove other owners, execute transactions, or upgrade the implementation
No multisig or threshold requirement - any single owner has full control

edge_cases.scenario: initialize() called with empty owners array
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should revert or set a meaningful state preventing re-initialization
edge_cases.actual_behavior: No-op: nextOwnerIndex stays 0, allowing another call to initialize() with different owners, potentially by an attacker
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If a proxy is deployed without using the factory (which enforces owners.length > 0), an attacker can front-run the legitimate initialize() call to take ownership. Factory-deployed wallets are not affected.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Some minimum owner count should be maintained
edge_cases.actual_behavior: All owners can be removed, permanently bricking the wallet with no way to recover access or funds
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: A compromised owner could remove all owners (including themselves) as a grief attack. The last owner removed cannot be re-added since no owner exists to authorize the operation.

edge_cases.scenario: MagicSpend nonce consumed before signature validation in _validateRequest
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Nonce should only be consumed for valid requests
edge_cases.actual_behavior: In withdraw(), _validateRequest consumes the nonce BEFORE isValidWithdrawSignature check. An attacker who obtains the withdraw request data (but not a valid signature) can front-run with the same nonce, burning it.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Front-running attack: attacker observes a pending withdraw() call in mempool, extracts the WithdrawRequest, and calls withdraw() first. The nonce is burned even though the signature check fails for the attacker. The legitimate user's subsequent call fails with InvalidNonce.

edge_cases.scenario: MagicSpend validatePaymasterUserOp nonce burned on failed signature
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Nonce only consumed for valid withdrawals
edge_cases.actual_behavior: _validateRequest is called before signature validation; nonce is permanently consumed even when sigFailed=true is returned to EntryPoint. The EntryPoint would reject the UserOp, but the nonce is already spent.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: An attacker can craft a UserOp referencing a victim's withdraw request (known from e.g., off-chain API). Even with an invalid signature, the nonce is consumed. The victim's legitimate withdrawal request becomes unusable, requiring a new signed request from the MagicSpend owner.

edge_cases.scenario: Multiple MagicSpend-sponsored UserOps in same bundle pass balance check individually
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Balance check should account for all pending withdrawals
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Each validatePaymasterUserOp checks address(this).balance independently. If the contract has 10 ETH and two UserOps each request 7 ETH, both pass validation. During postOp execution, the second transfer may fail.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Bundler or malicious actor could craft a bundle where multiple UserOps drain MagicSpend balance. The assert in postOp would be triggered if postOp is called with postOpReverted mode, consuming all remaining gas.

edge_cases.scenario: RIP-7212 precompile address 0x100 has deployed code on an unexpected chain
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Precompile returns valid P-256 signature verification result
edge_cases.actual_behavior: If an arbitrary contract exists at 0x100 on some chain, staticcall succeeds and returns data that may decode to uint256(1), causing false positive signature verification
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: On chains where 0x100 has arbitrary contract code, the staticcall might return data that passes the length > 0 check and decodes to 1, accepting forged signatures. FCL fallback would not execute.

edge_cases.actual_behavior: upgradeToAndCall is in canSkipChainIdValidation whitelist. A signed upgrade UserOp can be replayed on every chain where the wallet exists at the same address
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If the target implementation address doesn't exist or contains different code on another chain, the replay could brick wallets or introduce malicious implementations on other chains

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should check both x == 0 and y == 0 for identity point
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Only checks y == 0. A point on the curve with y = 0 (if one exists for P-256) would be treated as the identity. For P-256 with cofactor 1, such a point would have order 2, which is impossible since the group order is prime.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: For P-256 specifically, no point with y=0 exists on the curve because the group order is prime and > 2. So this is not exploitable for P-256 but would be a bug for other curves.

edge_cases.scenario: WebAuthn verify with requireUV set to false
edge_cases.expected_behavior: User verification provides stronger assurance of user intent
edge_cases.actual_behavior: CoinbaseSmartWallet._validateSignature calls WebAuthn.verify with requireUV: false, meaning user verification is never required
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: An authenticator that does not perform user verification (e.g., no biometric/PIN check) can still produce valid signatures. This reduces the security guarantee from the authenticator.

edge_cases.scenario: MagicSpend withdraw() allows withdrawal at exactly expiry timestamp
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Consistent behavior: either include or exclude the boundary
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Uses block.timestamp > expiry (allows at exact expiry). But validatePaymasterUserOp packs expiry into validationData where EntryPoint uses validUntil semantics (block.timestamp < validUntil). Inconsistent boundary handling.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Marginal timing inconsistency between direct withdraw and paymaster paths at the exact expiry second. Not practically exploitable.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Removed index could be reused to conserve index space
edge_cases.actual_behavior: nextOwnerIndex only increments; removed indices are permanently abandoned. Indices grow monotonically. SignatureWrapper.ownerIndex for a removed owner would return empty bytes, causing revert in _validateSignature.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: No direct exploitation, but over a very long wallet lifetime with many owner changes, ownerIndex grows unboundedly.

assumption_violations: MagicSpend assumes EntryPoint validates expiry from validationData - correct for ERC-4337 but relies on EntryPoint behavior
WebAuthn assumes authenticatorData is at least 33 bytes; shorter data causes out-of-bounds revert, not graceful failure
FCL assumes curve has prime order (cofactor 1) - true for P-256 but library comment warns against other curves
Factory assumes initialize() can only be called once due to nextOwnerIndex check - violated if called with empty owners array
MagicSpend postOp assumes actualGasCost <= maxGasCost, relying on EntryPoint invariant
WebAuthn relies on RIP-7212 precompile at 0x100 being either absent or correctly implemented; arbitrary code at that address breaks the verification
game_theory_risks: Nonce-burning grief attack on MagicSpend: attacker can burn a victim's withdrawal nonce by front-running with the same WithdrawRequest, requiring the MagicSpend owner to issue a new signed request
Single-owner takeover: any owner can unilaterally add themselves as sole owner and remove all others; no multisig protection
Cross-chain replay weapon: an attacker who compromises one chain's owner keys can replay owner changes and upgrades to all chains simultaneously
Bundler can refuse to include withdrawGasExcess() calls, temporarily locking user's excess ETH in MagicSpend
integration_risks: EntryPoint v0.6 specific: hardcoded EntryPoint address (0x5FF137D4b0FDCD49DcA30c7CF57E578a026d2789) means incompatibility with EntryPoint v0.7 or any future version
RIP-7212 precompile availability varies by chain; on chains without it, every WebAuthn verification falls back to FCL with higher gas cost
Solady SafeTransferLib.forceSafeTransferETH behavior may differ across EVM-compatible chains with different precompile or opcode support
MagicSpend relies on address(this).balance which can be manipulated via SELFDESTRUCT (deprecated but still functional pre-Cancun) or coinbase rewards
calculation_errors: No arithmetic overflow errors found in MagicSpend: Solidity 0.8.23 provides native overflow protection for non-assembly code
FCL assembly arithmetic uses mulmod/addmod which inherently handle modular arithmetic correctly for P-256 parameters
FCL ecZZ_mulmuladd_S_asm: at loop termination, the index wraps from 0 to 2^256-1 in assembly, but loop condition gt(minus_1, index) correctly exits since minus_1 == 2^256-1

token_semantics.notes: Primary asset for MagicSpend. Transferred via SafeTransferLib.safeTransferETH and forceSafeTransferETH. No ERC20 token accounting.

MagicSpend._withdraw supports generic ERC20 via SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer for ownerWithdraw and withdraw. MagicSpend.withdraw allows non-ETH assets but validatePaymasterUserOp rejects them (UnsupportedPaymasterAsset). Fee-on-transfer tokens would cause accounting discrepancies if amount received differs from amount specified.

invariants.name: nonce_uniqueness
invariants.description: Each (nonce, account) pair in MagicSpend can only be used once. Once _nonceUsed is set to true, it can never be reset.

invariants.name: owner_consistency
invariants.description: For every index where ownerAtIndex[index] is non-empty, isOwner[ownerAtIndex[index]] must be true. Violated temporarily if the same owner bytes could be at multiple indices (prevented by AlreadyOwner check).

invariants.name: nextOwnerIndex_monotonic
invariants.description: nextOwnerIndex only increases. Removing an owner does not decrement it.

invariants.name: withdrawableETH_conservation
invariants.description: The sum of all _withdrawableETH values should not exceed the contract's ETH balance minus funds reserved for gas. This invariant is checked at validation time (balance >= withdrawAmount) but multiple concurrent validations could violate it.

invariants.name: implementation_not_initializable
invariants.description: The implementation contract's constructor sets address(0) as owner, making nextOwnerIndex = 1, preventing re-initialization on the implementation itself.

invariants.name: signature_malleability_prevention
invariants.description: WebAuthn enforces s <= n/2 for secp256r1 signatures. ECDSA (secp256k1) malleability is handled by SignatureCheckerLib from Solady.
